title: Born Across From the Health Department
summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby in her home in Port Gibson, Mississippi, in December 2015, Leesco Guster recalls being born on Main Street across from the Health Department on March 26, 1936, delivered by a midwife, and being raised by a single mother who did domestic work and moved to Vicksburg after separating from her father.

title: Negro Street Was Busy
summary: She describes the odd grade-shuffling between Vicksburg schools, liking home economics over sports, and moving to Port Gibson in 1953, where the strip then called Negro Street, now Fair Street, was lined with joints and a cafe called Peaches and felt alive.

title: Colored Fountain and White Fountain
summary: Guster recalls absorbing race by instinct without it bothering her young, then growing to feel things needed to change, naming the colored and white fountains, and remembers living in the house rather than the basement of the white family she worked for before going to Chicago in the 1950s.

title: You Worked Yourself to Death
summary: She contrasts the punishing pace of Chicago, hopping the El and buses between jobs and working Sundays for extra pay, with a stint at Montgomery Ward cut short by her nerves, and recalls cleaning tile floors on her hands and owning her own house at sixteen with help from a sister who managed her Social Security check.

title: You Could Look Clear Under the House
summary: Guster describes her husband Harry Guster, a lumber contractor about twenty-four years her senior whom everyone in Claiborne County knew, and the run-down house he had bought for around twenty-five hundred dollars, so unfinished you could stand at the front and see clear underneath it to the back.

title: I Gave Him a Year to Think About It
summary: Returning on a three-week vacation from Chicago at nineteen, she told Harry she would not come back unless he married her and divorced his first wife, recalls the friction caused by his young son whose mother had expected Harry to take the boy back, and how she pushed Harry to keep paying the twenty-five then thirty dollars a month of support even when lumber work was scarce, until the boy's mother took him to Cleveland, Ohio and stopped collecting.

title: I Wanted People Registered to Vote
summary: She tells how Mr. Walcott let her work for his daughter Hillary, who had moved from Adena, Ohio, and explains that though she was not even an NAACP member, she wanted to vote, to register people, and to win a little recognition and a better job, hanging around McFatter's drugstore where Rudy Shields and the federal workers gathered.

title: Well, Lee, We Don't Need You Anymore
summary: Guster describes canvassing across Claiborne County after the federal registrars arrived, being seen standing out at registration, and her employer, who had come from Ohio and taken on the local whites' attitude, complaining she was tired of all that mess and politely letting Guster go, after which Guster walked home and kept going up to the drugstore every evening.

title: Brave, Wasn't Scared of Nothing
summary: She recalls meeting Rudy Shields on the street in front of the drugstore, a brave man who spoke up about what he knew and was doing what she wanted to do, and how she volunteered her car and a pistol while keeping the sickly Shields from marching or risking jail, sitting up with a shotgun while he stayed in her house, and insisting she was brave though she does not know where it came from.

title: We Lost All Our Work
summary: Guster describes the family living on a hundred-fourteen-dollar check and a small Social Security payment after both she and Harry lost their work, Harry having come into the area with Charles Evers but staying only long enough to sleep, and the patient canvassing she and Rudy did among people who had never voted, some going along and some too afraid.

title: He Had His Shotgun on the Truck
summary: She recounts her scariest moment, going out to Rocky Springs where the property owner waited with a shotgun racked across his pickup, telling them he did not want to lose what he had, and Rudy Shields, calling Harry Guster's name and telling her to just drive, sitting in the back with a pistol as they passed the man's truck at a one-way bridge.

title: We Had as High as Three
summary: Guster tells of putting up civil rights lawyers and workers in her home, including a sweet woman named Tuck Tate, with Lily Mae Williams as chairperson handling arrangements, doing it without pay though others spread that she was being paid, and explaining the gas donations she did collect and read aloud at meetings.

title: Count on Both Hands
summary: She reflects that she could never have passed the courthouse registration test herself, and names the handful of Black people registered before the movement, among them Mr. and Mrs. Colton, the Johnsons, Roscoe, and Junior Jones, so few you could almost count them on both hands.

title: I Put the Lord Behind
summary: Guster admits she stopped attending choir practice and could not pay her church dues because the meetings met when choir did, a wrong she says she would not repeat, and describes being put over the youth after Rudy organized them, taking the children to sing freedom songs at church.

title: They Took the Pulpit for a Play Place
summary: She names freedom songs the youth sang, including Ain't Gonna Let Nobody Turn Me Around and He's Got the Whole World in His Hands, recalls James Miller among the children, and objects to A.C. Garner wanting dances and to youngsters beating on the pulpit, which is what pushed her aside and led Reverend Dawson to step down.
keywords: Ain't Gonna Let Nobody Turn Me Around

title: He Was on the Radical Side
summary: Guster names good speakers like Mrs. Wales and Miss Julia Jones while counting herself and Mr. Nate among the quieter workers, explains that a speaker she found radical pushed a second boycott she opposed for punishing merchants like Claiborne Hardware who had already complied, and lists the first boycott's demands: the right to vote, recognition, and putting Black workers like a daughter or sister on the cash register, recalling Moselle hiring four Black workers.

title: He Asked Was I Tired of Living
summary: Guster recounts a man phoning during preparations for a banquet that Charles Evers was to attend, asking for Harry Guster and whether she was tired of living, identifying himself as a member of the KKK, and the later threats to burn her house and the place where Rudy was staying.

title: The Only Fatality Was the Mattress
summary: She describes a tense night under the shed when men were thought to be on the food-stand roof and a truck slowed as if to stop, Harry and the others keeping watch with shotguns, and how the next morning she accidentally fired a shotgun aimed down into the mattress, frightening Harry who thought she had killed herself.

title: He Was the One Who Brought the Klan
summary: Guster sizes up Charles Evers as a man who got right to the point and mostly talked while Rudy Shields was the main one on the ground, tells of the grocer Hudson saying he would do what was asked because it was for colored people, names a merchant named Jones she believed tried to bring the Klan in and was kept from opening his store for a year, recalls the federal registrar Bobby Gage rudely making them move their cars, and a sabotaged car given the wrong oil that ruined her motor during the boycott.

title: Don't Shoot, Just Scare
summary: Guster describes the enforcers organized at a green picnic table outside who handled people who broke the boycott, says she opposed their shooting into houses though she did not mind a good scare, recounts confronting a Priscilla Duck store with a pink house and glass showcase and a woman who kept letting the candy man come until Guster told her the business was over, and explains that the first boycott ended when stores added Black workers, floating her own idea of backing a white candidate since neither race can do without the other.

title: The Material Was Still on His Desk
topic: The Bank Loan and the House
summary: Guster recounts going to a banker named Cadman and then Drake for a loan to build a school addition onto her house, being refused because of the lawsuit over her property and then finding the loan already written up when the banker returned, overhearing a rumor at the bank that they meant to take her home, meeting the lawyer Bobby Gage with all her receipts to prove she was not behind, and crediting the house's survival to pushing Harry to start paying the twenty-five hundred dollars he still owed Mr. Shields's sister.

title: If You Were Black, No Deals
summary: Guster explains the lawsuit grew out of the boycott rather than any deal she made, recalls car salesmen Buster Barber and a man named Davis testifying they had no dealings with her, says the merchants put the names of everyone with property on the suit including old people like John Ellis who only signed bonds, and recounts Eddie Walls proudly answering in court that his race was oatmeal.

title: We Need Black and White Together
topic: Dusty Jackson and the Movement's Meaning
summary: She says she testified little, questioning how anyone could be sued for money the merchants would never have gotten anyway, recalls being in town when Nat Dusty Jackson was killed though she did not march for it, and reflects that the movement showed people they could come together for a while and accomplished something even as much was lost, arguing that Black and white need each other rather than doing to one another what was once done to them.

title: More for the Young People
summary: She says she worked not so much for herself as for the young people coming along, recalls trying to back Thomas Russell, whom she calls her backbone, for office because he refused to take what was meant for white officials, declines ever running herself, and tells young people to speak up for themselves but to work, not just talk.

title: I'm Not Going to Put No Lie Out There
summary: Guster recounts Kenneth Ross asking her for ten names for the Port Gibson mural, going far back among the people she knew, refusing to name women she felt only talked or to claim credit for herself, and praising Ross as smart while contrasting him with a man who collected church money for a museum that never came and turned it over to the NAACP.

title: Why Couldn't They Get Somebody From Here
summary: She compares the courthouse civil rights exhibit to the meetings, honors workers like George Henry and Reverend Dawson who labored in the heat, and resents that Margot, appointed president of the Walmart NAACP chapter because of who she was and where she came from, was set over local people who had done the struggle, while Guster refused to pay the twenty-five dollars.

title: We Were Good Friends in the Movement
summary: Guster says she is proud of her work though she did not do it for show, recalls George urging her to take in the ailing Rudy Shields and her telling him she could not, since she had a husband and they had been good friends only in the movement, and mentions a daughter of a man Rudy stayed with in Yazoo trying to write about him, before the interview closes.
